coredata.dc:title: Tunable photoacoustic and fluorescence imaging of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xuan
Guo, Huazhang
Chen, Changhan
Quan, Biao
Zeng, Zhiming
Xu, Jing
Chen, Zeyu
Wang, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Photoacoustic-fluorescence imaging is an emerging medical imaging technology developed in recent years that has shown immense application potential for specialties that depend on optical diagnoses, such as ophthalmology, otorhinolaryngology, and dermatology, etc., owing to its advantages of real-time, fast, and high sensitivity. Currently, photoacoustic (PA) probes mainly accumulate in the tumor region via neovascularization leakage and poor lymphatic drainage, which phenomenon refers to as the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. However, signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) of label free PA imaging are limited by the low tumor accumulation of PA probes. Beside, the modification process of PA probes is complex. Here, we designed and synthesized a new whole series of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) through a molecular fusion strategy. The synthesized N-CQDs displayed narrow size distribution and average sizes of ∼1.50 nm. Meanwhile, the N-CQDs exhibited tunable optical capability via the mass ratio changes of precursor and catalyst. Finally, the N-CQDs are applied as the bioimaging probes, which demonstrated fast imaging velocity, rapid metabolism process, and great EPR effect leading to better accumulation in the tumor region. We hereby offer a guideline to design advanced N-CQDs for both fluorescence imaging and PA imaging through a molecular fusion strategy. Given this strategy, the N-CQDs may possess both optimum fluorescence intensity and PA efficiency, which allows more comprehensive, high quality and accurate tumor imaging in vivo.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Photoacoustic imaging
Fluorescence imaging
Enhanced permeability and retention effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photoacoustic imaging of N-CQDs stock solution
Photoacoustic imaging of N-CQDs in vivo
Application of N-CQDs in fluorescence imaging
Application of N-CQDs in photoacoustic imaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The project was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21901154 ), and the Shanghai Pujiang Program (21PJD022).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematics of synthesis of N-CQDs with tunable optical capability via the ratio changes of 1,2,4-triaminobenzene and NaOH. When the ratio of n(NaOH)/n(1,2,4-triaminobenzene) =1.47, the N-CQDs-2 is endowed with robust fluorescence imaging capability by its long-wavelength emission behavior. When the ratio of n(NaOH)/n(1,2,4-triaminobenzene) =4.90, the N-CQDs-5 exhibits great PAI properties with an optimal emission peak at 595 when excited at 470 nm. Given the advantages of narrow size distribution and an ultra-small average size of ∼1.50 nm, the synthesized N-CQDs show a great EPR effect leading to better accumulation in the tumor region, as well as the advantages of rapid nuclear entry, rapid imaging, and rapid metabolism. With the above qualities, the N-CQDs show immense application potential for specialties that depend on optical diagnoses, such as ophthalmology, otorhinolaryngology, dermatology, etc.

(a) TEM image and lateral size distribution of N-CQDs-2. (b) TEM image and lateral size distribution of N-CQDs-5. (c) The HRTEM image of N-CQDs-2. (d) The HRTEM image of N-CQDs-5. (e) XRD patterns of N-CQDs-2 and N-CQDs-5. (f) Raman spectra of N-CQDs-2 and N-CQDs-5.

(a) FTIR spectra of N-CQDs-2 and N-CQDs-5. (b) Survey XPS spectra of N-CQDs-2 and N-CQDs-5. (c) High-resolution N1s spectrum of N-CQDs-2. (d) High-resolution O1s spectrum of N-CQDs-2. (e) High-resolution N1s spectrum of N-CQDs-5. (f) High-resolution O1s spectrum of N-CQDs-5.

(a) The zeta-potential of different molar ratios of NaOH and 1,2,4-triaminobenzene and corresponding photographs of N-CQDs under daylight (left) and UV light (right, 365 nm). (b) The structural formulas of N-CQDs-2 and N-CQDs-5. (c) The UV-vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs. (d) The PL spectra of N-CQDs. (e) The PL and PLE spectra of N-CQDs-2. (f) The PL emission spectra of N-CQDs-2 under different excitation wavelengths. (g) The PL and PLE spectra of N-CQDs-5. (h) The PL emission spectra of N-CQDs-5 under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Confocal fluorescence images of living FaDu cells with N-CQDs excited at 514 nm. (b) Relative fluorescence intensity of different N-CQDs in living FaDu cells. (c) Relative fluorescence intensity distribution (cell, cytoplasm, and nucleus) of N-CQDs-2 in living FaDu cells. (d) Confocal fluorescence images of N-CQDs-2 incubated with living FaDu cells for 5, 15, and 30 min, respectively (excited at 514 nm). (e) Relative fluorescence intensity of different incubation times (5, 15, and 30 min) in living FaDu cells. (f) In vivo fluorescence images after different times of N-CQDs-2 injection (excitation: 490 nm; emission: 560 nm). (g) Relative fluorescence intensity after different times of N-CQDs-2 injection. (h) Comparison of fluorescence intensity before and after N-CQDs-2 injection. *P ≤ 0.05 by one-sample t-test. (i) In vivo fluorescence images of tumor dissected 30 min after injection of N-CQDs-2 and N.S respectively (a) and tumor, heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney dissected 30 min after injection of N-CQDs-2 (b).

Photoacoustic imaging properties of N-CQDs. (a) Photoacoustic imaging of N-CQDs stock solution at excitation wavelengths 470 and 560 nm respectively. (b) PA intensity of N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (excitation: 400, 470, and 560 nm, respectively). (c) PA intensity of N-CQDs-5 at different excitation wavelengths (350–650 nm). (d) In vivo photoacoustic images after different times of N-CQDs-5 injection (excitation: 470 and 560 nm respectively). (e) Relative photoacoustic intensity after different times of N-CQDs-5 injection in vivo (excitation: 470 and 560 nm respectively). (f) Comparison of fluorescence intensity before and after N-CQDs-5 injection (excitation: 470 and 560 nm respectively). *P ≤ 0.05 by one-sample t-test.

head.ce:title: Tunable photoacoustic and fluorescence imaging of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Ophthalmology and Otolaryngology, Xiangya Hospital, Central South University, Changsha 41008, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoacoustic-fluorescence imaging is an emerging medical imaging technology developed in recent years that has shown immense application potential for specialties that depend on optical diagnoses, such as ophthalmology, otorhinolaryngology, and dermatology, etc., owing to its advantages of real-time, fast, and high sensitivity. Currently, photoacoustic (PA) probes mainly accumulate in the tumor region via neovascularization leakage and poor lymphatic drainage, which phenomenon refers to as the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. However, signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) of label free PA imaging are limited by the low tumor accumulation of PA probes. Beside, the modification process of PA probes is complex. Here, we designed and synthesized a new whole series of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) through a molecular fusion strategy. The synthesized N-CQDs displayed narrow size distribution and average sizes of ∼1.50 nm. Meanwhile, the N-CQDs exhibited tunable optical capability via the mass ratio changes of precursor and catalyst. Finally, the N-CQDs are applied as the bioimaging probes, which demonstrated fast imaging velocity, rapid metabolism process, and great EPR effect leading to better accumulation in the tumor region. We hereby offer a guideline to design advanced N-CQDs for both fluorescence imaging and PA imaging through a molecular fusion strategy. Given this strategy, the N-CQDs may possess both optimum fluorescence intensity and PA efficiency, which allows more comprehensive, high quality and accurate tumor imaging in vivo.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence (FL) imaging and Photoacoustic (PA) imaging have great application prospects in light triggered diagnostic technology given their outstanding performances in cancer diagnosis and imaging guided therapy. However, although FL imaging shows high sensitivity, its performance is restricted due to a lack of good spatial resolution and the limitation of penetration depth. To overcome it, it is necessary to seek out more appropriate imaging probes such as probes combining PA imaging methods. Certainly, PA imaging is a burgeoning noninvasive and nonionizing biomedical imaging modality for preclinical and clinical investigations, integrating the advantages of optical imaging and ultrasound imaging with rich contrast, real-time correlation, relatively high spatial resolution, and clinically relevant tissue penetration depths [1–3]. The excitation of a nanosecond laser pulse, while irradiating biological tissues of interest in the process of imaging, can induce propagation of protons inside the tissue or contrast agents and produce a localized thermally induced pressure rise in a short space of time that emits broadband acoustic waves, which can be detected by ultrasound transducers and reconstructed by algorithms [4,5]. The PA probes or contrast agents are critical in determining the sensitivity and specificity of PA imaging, such as biomolecular probes, small molecular probes, nanoparticle (NP)-based probes, etc. [6–9]. Current PA probes mainly rely on the enhanced accumulation in the tumor region due to neovascularization leakage and poor lymphatic drainage, which phenomenon refers to as the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect [4,10,11]. Therefore, it is urgent to exploit the emerging probes to achieve high SNR ratios and rid complex modifications [12–14].

The NPs provide an ideal platform for developing novel probes for tumor microenvironment imaging given their size advantage, thus possessing a great EPR effect [7,9]. Among the multiple NP-based probes, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) remain to be indispensable carbon nanomaterials widely applied in biological PA imaging modalities due to their tunability of fluorescence, rich surface charges and chemical groups, low cost, and excellent biocompatibility. At present, a great quantity of CQDs were synthesized by chemical reagents [15–27] (including polythiophene phenylpropionic acid, citric acid, phenylenediamine, 4-vinyl phenylboric acid, glutathione, petroleum coke,) and natural biomass [28–37] (including garlic, spinach, sesame, corn bracts, crab shells, egg white, and bee pollen) for wide investigation of potential use as PA probes. Nevertheless, the synthetic complexity and high cost of polythiophene benzoic acid [17], the repeatability of biological matrix preparation [21], and the main absorption band of the CQDs in the ultraviolet to the green region [38–42] greatly hinder its practical application. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop CQDs with multimodality functions, which can promote further development of CQDs in the biomedical fields of fluorescence imaging and photoacoustic imaging.

Herein, we designed and synthesized a new series of nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs). The morphology and structure of the synthesized N-CQDs were investigated and analyzed. The N-CQDs exhibited tunable optical capability via the mass ratio changes of precursor and catalyst. Beside, the N-CQDs were applied as the bioimaging probes, which demonstrated fast imaging velocity, rapid metabolism process, and great EPR effect leading to better accumulation in the tumor region. After detailed theoretical calculation and experimental validation, we hereby offer a guideline to design advanced N-CQDs for both fluorescence imaging and PA imaging through molecular fusion strategy (Fig. 1 ). Given this strategy, the N-CQDs may possess both optimum fluorescence intensity and PA efficiency, which allows a more comprehensive, high quality and accurate tumor imaging in vivo.

This method is improved based on the articles published in our group [23]. 1,2,4-triaminobenzene (0.05 g) and NaOH (0, 0.015, 0.025, 0.035, 0.050, and 0.075 g) were dissolved in 10 mL H2O, the mixture solution was then transferred to a 25 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with PTFE and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. The reaction was cooled to room temperature naturally. Insoluble carbon products were filtered by a 0.22 µm microporous membrane, and then the solvent was eliminated by rotary evaporation and transferred to normal saline for the study of all-optical properties. The unreacted precursor small molecules were removed by dialysis using a dialysis bag for 7 days and dried at 80 °C to obtain black solid powder (labeled N-CQDs-1, N-CQDs-2, N-CQDs-3, N-CQDs-4, N-CQDs-5, and N-CQDs-6), and were further used for structural characterization. The concentration of N-CQDs in the solution was concentrated by the following methods: the 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes are weighed (3 in parallel). Then 1 mL of N-CQDs solution was added into the centrifuge tubes using pipette guns, and the mass was weighed after drying. The mass concentration before and after drying (average value) is the concentration of the sample to be used (unit: mg/mL).

FaDu cells were used for confocal imaging of N-CQDs (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6). Approximately 1 ⅹ 105 FaDu cells were seeded into culture dishes (diameter: 35 mm dish with 14 mm bottom well) and cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle (DMEM) medium (Gibco, USA) with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (Hyclone, USA) at 37 °C under 5% CO2/95% air. All cells were incubated for 24 h until 80% confluence was reached. Before confocal imaging, N-CQDs aqueous solutions were added to the culture medium at the final concentration of 5 mg/L, and 1 mL of the mixture solution was added to the FaDu cell culture dish. After incubation of different duration (5 min, 15 min, and 30 min), FaDu cells were washed twice with PBS solution to remove excess N-CQDs and were then observed and photographed under a confocal microscope.

The toxicity of N-CQDs-2 and N-CQDs-5 was evaluated by MTT assays. FaDu cells were seeded in 96-well plates (approximately 5 × 103 cells per well) in 200 μL of DMEM for 12 h. After cells completely adhered to the plate wall, 100 μL of fresh culture medium containing N-CQDs-2 and N-CQDs-5 in various concentrations (final concentrations were 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mg/L, respectively) was added in replacement of the old culture medium. The mixture of FaDu cells and N-CQDs were incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2/95% air for 24 h and 48 h. Then, 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. The old culture medium containing MTT was removed carefully, and FaDu cells were washed 2,3 times with PBS solution and were then substituted with 150 μL DMSO solution to dissolve the obtained crystals. After oscillation for 10 min, the absorbance of each well was measured at 490 nm using a microplate reader (Biotek, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoacoustic imaging of N-CQDs stock solution
ce:section.ce:para: N-CQDs stock liquid was placed in a plastic tube of 0.5 mm inner diameter, then the tube was sealed with a sealing film to prevent leakage of liquid during the experiment. The tube of N-CQDs stock liquid was placed in a water tank using a self-made test model. After the output wavelength of the laser was set (model: SpitLight 600-OPO-355), a Verasonics Vantage 256 channel ultrasound system (Verasonics Inc, Kirkland, WA, USA), and a linear array transducer L11–5v (Verasonics Inc, Kirkland, WA, USA) with a center frequency at 8 MHz and standard elevation focus at 18 mm were used to collect the photoacoustic signal. The signal was transmitted to a computer through a PIC line for real-time reconstruction and display. The imaging area of the optical fiber and the ultrasonic transducer was adjusted to position the N-CQDs stock liquid by controlling the triaxial displacement table, then photoacoustic signals and images were recorded, and 100 frames of photoacoustic data were collected. The collected 100 frames of photoacoustic data were then averaged on MATLAB to obtain the final image and photoacoustic signal strength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoacoustic imaging of N-CQDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: C57BL/6 mice xenograft tumor models were established. Mice were depilated for exposure to the tumor site, then anesthetized with 1% sodium pentobarbital solution at a dose of 50 mg/kg. After successful anesthesia, mice were fixed on a 45° acrylic plate in the water tank. Different wavelength of the laser was set, then a Verasonics Vantage 256 channel ultrasound system and a linear array transducer L11–5v with a center frequency at 8 MHz and standard elevation focus at 18 mm were used to receive the photoacoustic signal. The signal was then transmitted to a computer through a PIC line for real-time reconstruction and display. Six mice were divided into two groups: The N-CQDs-5 group (experimental group) and the PBS group (control group). The experimental group was injected with N-CQDs-5 at the dose of 30 mg/kg via the tail vein, and the control group was injected with PBS via the tail vein. Photoacoustic images of mice were collected and processed consistent with the imaging of N-CQDs stock solution, at 5, 15, 30, and 60 min after injection, respectively.

C57BL/6 mice xenograft tumor models were established. IVIS imaging system was used to monitor the enrichment and metabolism of N-CQDs-2 in tumor sites in mice. Mice were depilated to fully expose the tumor site and were anesthetized with 2% isoflurane. Fluorescence images of all mice were collected using the IVIS imaging system before injection. Then, six mice were divided into two groups: The N-CQDs-2 group (experimental group) and the PBS group (control group). The experimental group was injected with N-CQDs-2 at the dose of 30 mg/kg through the tail vein, and the control group was injected with PBS via the tail vein. Fluorescence images of mice were collected and analyzed via the IVIS imaging system at 5, 15, 30, 60, 120, and 240 min after injection, respectively. In addition, fluorescence images of metabolic correlated organs dissected from these mice, including the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, kidneys, and tumors, were also collected and analyzed using the IVIS imaging system. The fluorescence intensity was expressed as the average radiation efficiency in [p/sec/cm2/sr]/[mW/cm2].

The heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor tissues were harvested, the tissue specimens were then fixed with 4% neutral buffered formalin for 24 h, processed routinely into paraffin, sectioned, and then stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). Tissue sections were observed using a digital pathology scanning system.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observation of the samples was measured by Hitachi HT7700 from Japan. XRD patterns were obtained by Rigaku (SmartLab-II, Japan). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) was obtained by JEOL JEM 2100F from Shiyanjia Lab. Raman spectra were assessed using Cora 5001 (Anton Paar OptoTec GmbH), which was implemented utilizing 532 nm as the excitation light source. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were characterized by applying an FTIR absorption spectrometer (TGA8000, PerkinElmer, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were gathered by making use of the Scientific Compass Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. The optical properties were characterized by using a Hitachi 7000 and a U-3010 spectrophotometer, respectively. The 3D spectra were represented by HORIBA Duetta. Time-resolved PL spectrum was measured by Horiba FluoroMax Plus. Zeta potentials of N-CQDs were measured at 25 °C using a ZS90 Malvern Zetasizer Nano. All N-CQDs samples were photographed using a camera (D7000, Nikon). Cell imaging was taken using a confocal microscope (Zeiss LSM880, Germany). Toxicity evaluation was measured by employing a microplate reader (Biotek, USA). PAI was taken via Verasonics Vantage 256 channel ultrasound system (Verasonics Inc, Kirkland, WA, USA), laser (model: SpitLight 600-OPO-355), linear array transducer L11–5v (Verasonics Inc, Kirkland, WA, USA). Fluorescence imaging in vivo was taken via an IVIS imaging system (Spectrum, PerkinElmer, USA). H&E stained tissue sections were observed utilizing a digital pathology scanning system (KF-PRO-005-EX, KFBio).

The N-CQDs were prepared by a one-pot modified hydrothermal method with 1,2,4-triaminobenzene and NaOH [23] (Table S1). As shown in their morphological characterization of the TEM images (Fig. 2 a and b), the N-CQDs-2 and N-CQDs-5 were primarily spherical and well dispersed in an aqueous solution. All their size distribution is narrow, and the average sizes are 1.53 and 1.50 nm, respectively. The phenomenon indicated that the size of the produced CQDs is only slightly affected despite the changes in their synthesis conditions. And their crystal plane spacing is 0.21 nm in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images, which correspond to the (100) graphite plane (Fig. 2c and d). The surface structures of N-CQDs-2 and N-CQDs-5 were characterized by XRD and Raman spectroscopy. X-ray diffraction pattern (Fig. 2e) shows that there is the same obvious peak of all the samples with [002] interplanar spacing. The interlayer spacing is 3.6 Å (2θ=24.95°) and 3.5 Å (2θ=25 for N-CQDs-2 and N-CQDs-5, respectively. Raman spectra further confirmed the surface structure of N-CQDs (Fig. 2f). It is seen that the G-band peak (∼1574 cm−1) of the sp2 graphitic carbon structure and the D-band peak (∼1348 cm−1) of the sp3 hybrid carbon structure, which represent ordered and disordered arrangement, respectively. The intensity ratio reflects the defect intensity of the N-CQD. The I D/I G intensity ratios of N-CQDs-2 and N-CQDs-5 were 1.10 and 0.84, respectively, which indicated that N-CQDs-2 had more defects than N-CQDs-5. The result is in agreement with their XRD results.

The surface functional groups of the samples were characterized by the FTIR and XPS measurements. According to the FTIR spectra (Fig. 3 a), N-CQDs-2 and N-CQDs-5 have the same stretching vibration peaks, which are O-H (3330 cm−1), N-H (3217 cm−1), C=O (1622 cm−1), C=C (1479 cm−1), C-OH (1230 cm−1), and C-N (1134 cm−1). The XPS analysis further verified the atom ratio change of these groups. As is shown in their total XPS spectra (Fig. 3b and Table S2), all the N-CQDs comprised C, N, and O atoms. The outcomes of the elemental analysis also demonstrated that the C content of the N-CQDs-2 and N-CQDs-5 was 72.33 at% and 72.93 at%, respectively, indicating that the carbonization degrees of N-CQDs-5 is higher than that of N-CQDs-2 and agreeing with the result of their Raman data. The high-resolution C1s spectra of N-CQDs-2 and N-CQDs-5 can be divided into four different types of C species: C-C/C=C (284.80 eV), C-N (285.67 eV), C-O (286.42 eV), and C=O (288.82 eV) (Fig. S1). As is shown in Fig. 3c, the N1s spectrum of N-CQDs-2 has four peaks at 398.69, 399.72, 400.83, and 402.06 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, amino N, graphitic N, and N-O, respectively [23,43]. In contrast, there are only three peaks of the N1s spectrum of N-CQDs-5 (Fig. 3e). The elimination of N-O groups may be caused by the dehydration carbonization in their graphitization process. The two kinds of N-CQDs in O1s spectra (Fig. 3d and f) can be seen in the three characteristic peaks of O-C, O=C, and O-N. Therefore, the XPS data confirmed that all the N-CQDs consist of the functional groups of -NH2, -OH, -COOH, and -N-OH on their surface.

The optical properties of N-CQDs aqueous solutions were deeply explored via the condition regulations of the hydrothermal method. With the increased ratio of n(NaOH)/n(1,2,4-triaminobenzene), the color of the resulting N-CQDs in the saline phase directly crosses from blue to orange (Fig. 4 a). When the ratio of n(NaOH)/n(1,2,4-triaminobenzene) =1.47, the charge of the solution becomes a positive charge of 11.22 mV, and the others are negative charges (Fig. 4a and Table S3), which provide a theoretical foundation for the positively charged CQDs into the nucleus for bioimaging applications [16]. According to the analysis of morphology and structural characterization, the possible structure of the N-CQDs can be seen in Fig. 4b. In Fig. 4c, the absorption region of CQDs is very broad with the longest absorption wavelength of even 750 nm, extending from the UV region to the Vis region. The typical absorption peak of CQDs is mainly located at about 430 nm, whereas that of N-CQDs-2 redshifts to 500 nm due to the unique structure of N-CQDs-2, which corresponds to the XPS results. The fluorescence performance of the N-CQDs is displayed in Fig. 4d. The max emission wavelength of N-CQDs-1 is 405 nm, which agrees with its blue fluorescence color under the excitation of 365 UV light in Fig. 4a.

Moreover, the other N-CQDs emit yellow light and the only N-CQDs-2 emits the bright orange light under the UV light source of 365 nm, whose max emission wavelength ranges from 580 nm to 605 nm. As shown in Fig. 4e, the optimal emission and excitation wavelengths of the N-CQDs-2 are 595 and 400 nm, respectively. Fig. 4f showed the fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs-2 excited by different excitation wavelengths. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs-2 first increases with the excitation wavelength and then achieves its highest at 400 nm, whereas the optimum emission wavelength is 595 nm, elucidating that the N-CQDs-2 had no excitation dependence behaviors. On the contrary, N-CQDs-5 emits normal yellow light when irradiated under a UV light source at 365 nm. And the optimal emission and excitation peaks are 595 and 470 nm, respectively (Fig. 4g and h). Compared with other CQDs at different excitation wavelengths, its optimal emission wavelengths changed with obvious excitation dependence behaviors (Figs. S2 and S3). Moreover, the three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence spectroscopy analysis of N-CQDs were carried out in Fig. S4. Interestingly, only two excitation-independent PL peaks of N-CQDs-2 were observed, and the others have three excitation-independent PL peaks. That may be the factor that distinguishes N-CQDs-2 from other N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CQDs in fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the differences in fluorescence properties of N-CQDs, FaDu cells were incubated with N-CQDs for 5 min, then pictures were taken under a confocal microscope (LSM 880, Jena), and analyzed using ZEISS ZEN microscope software. Results showed a significantly higher average fluorescence intensity over 40 in cells incubated with N-CQDs-2, which was about 40 times higher than that of cells incubated with N-CQDs-1, and twice that of cells incubated with N-CQDs-3. Moreover. The average fluorescence intensity in cells incubated with N-CQDs-3, N-CQDs-4, and N-CQDs-5 were all below 5, indicating that N-CQDs-2 had the best fluorescence imaging capacity (Fig. 5 a–c). Based on the above results, N-CQDs-2 was selected for further experiments regarding fluorescence imaging property testing.

To further explore the approximate time needed for N-CQDs-2 to enter the nucleus, FaDu cells were incubated with N-CQDs-2 for 5, 15, and 30 min respectively. A considerably high average fluorescence intensity was found in cells incubated for 5 and 30 min, while the fluorescence intensity of cells incubated for 15 min exceeded 80, exhibiting the strongest fluorescence intensity amongst the three, indicating that N-CQDs-2 entered the nucleus within 5 min, and the signal of fluorescence imaging enhanced with the extension of incubation time from 5 to 15 min, but weakened after an incubation time between 15 and 30 min. (Fig. 5d and e).

To further investigate the fluorescence imaging ability of N-CQDs-2 in vivo, a subcutaneous transplanted tumor model was established using Meer cells and C57BL/6 mice. When the tumor volume reached 50 mm3, N-CQDs-2 (30 mg/kg) was injected via the tail vein. Real-time in vivo imaging revealed a considerably strong fluorescence signal at the tumor site at 5 min, indicating that N-CQDs-2 could be well delivered to the tumor site within 5 min given enhanced permeability and retention ability due to its ultra-small size (1.5 nm), and achieve ideal quality fluorescence imaging of the tumor in a short level of time [44]. (Fig. 5f). Furthermore, radiant efficiency reached the peak at 15 min, which was significantly higher than pre-injection, and rapidly dropped to a level close to that seen at 5 min, which was consistent with in vitro fluorescence imaging results. Radiant efficiency continued to decrease gradually and was about half of the peak value at 120 min, demonstrating a rapid metabolism process. (Fig. 5g,h). Main metabolic organs along with the tumor were dissected 15 min after injection of N-CQDs-2 and N.S. respectively. Radiant efficiency was significantly stronger in tumors of mice injected with N-CQDs-2, reaffirming the excellent fluorescence imaging capability of N-CQDs-2. In mice injected with N-CQDs-2, radiant efficiency was significantly stronger in tumor tissue and the hepatic artery, a low level of radiant efficiency was exhibited in the liver, kidney, and lung tissue, and was hardly found in heart and spleen tissue, further verifying well accumulation in tumor region given great EPR effect due to the ultra-small size of N-CQDs-2. In addition, to clarify the toxicity of N-CQDs-2, MTT and HE staining of the organs of mice were performed. These results showed that N-CQDs-2 had no obvious toxicity [45,46] (Fig. S5a and c). These results showed that N-CQDs-2 had the advantages of rapid imaging and rapid metabolism with no negative effect on the research targets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CQDs in photoacoustic imaging
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the differences in photoacoustic imaging properties of N-CQDs, a device and algorithm were built and designed respectively to test the photoacoustic intensity of N-CQDs. A photoacoustic imaging system was used to test and analyze the photoacoustic intensity of N-CQDs at excitation wavelengths of 400 nm, 470 nm, and 560 nm respectively (Fig. 6 a). In general, N-CQDs-2, N-CQDs-4, and N-CQDs-5 all exhibited ideal photoacoustic intensity at all excitation wavelengths, however, an overall higher photoacoustic intensity of all N-CQDs was observed at 470 nm, and N-CQDs-5 exhibited the highest photoacoustic intensity amongst the six at 470 nm, suggesting that N-CQDs-5 had an ideal photoacoustic imaging ability to show the strongest photoacoustic signal at the most suitable excitation wavelength (Fig. 6a–c). Therefore, we selected N-CQDs-5 for further experiments regarding photoacoustic imaging property testing.

To further observe the photoacoustic imaging ability of N-CQDs-5 in vivo, a subcutaneous transplanted tumor model was constructed using Meer cells and C57BL/6 mice. When the tumor volume reached 50 mm3, N-CQDs-5 (30 mg/kg) was injected via the tail vein. Real-time in vivo photoacoustic imaging at excitation wavelengths of 470 nm and 560 nm both revealed a considerably strong photoacoustic signal at the tumor site at 5 min, following a signal intensity peak at 15 min, which was significantly higher than pre-injection, and rapidly dropped to a low level at 30 min and continued to gradually decrease to a level close to pre-injection at 60 min. Photoacoustic imaging results of N-CQDs-5 were essentially in agreement with fluorescence imaging results of N-CQDs-2, reconfirming the consistent imaging advantages of N-CQDs including fast imaging velocity, rapid metabolism process, and great EPR effect due to its ultra-small size leading to better accumulation in the tumor region [47,48] (Fig. 6d–f). In addition, MTT and HE staining of organs of mice were performed to clarify the toxicity of N-CQDs-5. These results showed that N-CQDs-2 had no obvious toxicity [49] (Fig. S5b and c).

In summary, a new series of N-CQDs were designed and synthesized through a molecular fusion strategy. The synthesized N-CQDs exhibited narrow size distribution and an average size of ∼1.50 nm. All the N-CQDs consist of the functional groups of -NH2, -OH, -COOH, and -N-OH on their surface. More importantly, the N-CQDs exhibited tunable optical capability via the mass ratio changes of precursor and catalyst. When the ratio of n(NaOH)/n(1,2,4-triaminobenzene) =1.47, the charge of the N-CQDs-2 solution becomes a positive charge of 11.22 mV, and the N-CQDs-2 emits the bright orange light under the UV light source of 365 nm, whose max emission wavelength ranges from 580 nm to 605 nm. The emission wavelength is 595 nm, which exhibits the excitation-independent property of N-CQDs-2 with a wide range of wavelengths. The excellent optical behavior endows the N-CQDs-2 with robust fluorescence imaging performance, which has achieved ideal quality fluorescence imaging of the tumor in 5 min. On the other hand, When the ratio of n(NaOH)/n(1,2,4-triaminobenzene) =4.90, the charge of the N-CQDs-5 solution becomes a negative charge of −13.07 mV, and the N-CQDs-5 emits normal yellow light when irradiated under a UV light source at 365 nm. And the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are 470 and 595 nm, respectively. Most notably, the N-CQDs-5 when applied as PA probes, demonstrated fast imaging velocity, rapid metabolism process, and great EPR effect leading to better accumulation in the tumor region. We hereby offer a guideline to design advanced N-CQDs for both fluorescence imaging and PA imaging through a molecular fusion strategy. Given this strategy, the N-CQDs may possess both optimum fluorescence intensity and PA efficiency, which allows more comprehensive, high quality and accurate tumor imaging in vivo.

Xuan Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Huazhang Guo: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Changhan Chen: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Biao Quan: Visualization, Investigation. Zhiming Zeng: Visualization, Investigation. Jing Xu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Zeyu Chen: Supervision, Investigation. Liang Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21901154), and the Shanghai Pujiang Program (21PJD022).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.apmt.2022.101706.
